main_summary.summary: Dr. Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson), interviewed September 14, 2011 by Joe Mosnier on the University of Florida campus in Gainesville, recounts her path from Spelman College student to SNCC project director for Laurel, Mississippi during the 1964 Freedom Summer. Recruited into SNCC organizing circles in Atlanta despite Spelman College's prohibition on student political activity, Simmons drew on classes with Staughton Lynd, Howard Zinn, and Vincent Harding at the Mennonite House. After volunteering for the Freedom Summer Freedom School curriculum, she was sent to Laurel with James 'Jimmy' Garrett and Lester McKinney, where Mrs. Eberhart Spinks and Mrs. Carrie Clayton hosted the project. When McKinney was forced out of Jones County, James Forman named her project director. Simmons stayed sixteen months, running Freedom Schools, mock voter registration, an MFDP chapter, and adult literacy. After mass arrest at the Mississippi State Fairgrounds following a Jackson demonstration, Forman moved her to New York Friends of SNCC work, then to Atlanta to manage Julian Bond's re-election campaign, which became the SNCC Atlanta Project based in Vine City. She co-authored what became the Black Power position paper, was expelled from SNCC with the Atlanta Project, worked on COINTELPRO documentation at the American Friends Service Committee, and converted to Sufism under M.R. Bawa Muhaiyaddeen.
key_themes: Women in SNCC leadership and sexual-harassment policy
Religious-philosophical evolution toward nonviolence via Sufism
main_summary.historical_significance:
Simmons is one of the few SNCC Freedom Summer project directors who was Black, female, and college-age in 1964; her sixteen-month tenure in Laurel, Mississippi makes her an unusually long-serving local-project leader at a site too dangerous for white volunteers. Her testimony provides primary-source detail on the Laurel Project specifically (Mrs. Eberhart Spinks, Mrs. Carrie Clayton, Mr. Richardson, the Masonite Corporation union connection, the two fire-bombed offices) that is sparsely documented elsewhere in the SNCC literature.

The interview also contributes a participant account of the authorship of the SNCC Atlanta Project Black Power position paper, naming it as group-authored from Vine City discussions transcribed by Simmons and two other women from a Wollensak tape recorder, rather than originating with Stokely Carmichael alone. Her later American Friends Service Committee work investigating COINTELPRO, the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission, and the Fred Hampton assassination contributes oral-history material drawn from interviews she herself conducted with American Indian Movement, Brown Berets, Black Panther Party, and Rodolfo 'Corky' Gonzales-affiliated organizers.

chapters.title: Spelman, SNCC, and Atlanta Mentors
chapters.summary: Simmons describes her first impressions of the Atlanta SNCC office as a Spelman College student: James Forman at the center of activity, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, Japanese American colleagues, and Ella Baker observed at gatherings. She names Staughton Lynd, Howard Zinn, and Vincent Harding at the Mennonite House as the white professors who pulled her toward engagement while she risked her scholarship by violating Spelman College's prohibition on student political activity.

chapters.title: The Pull Toward Mississippi
chapters.summary: Simmons recounts the family and college pressures against involvement, the impact of John Lewis and the Nashville staff, and Willie 'Mukasa' Ricks's challenge that degrees would not change Black status. She describes the terror of being assigned to Laurel after the disappearance of Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner shattered her belief that white volunteers' presence would deter killings.
related_events: Disappearance of Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner (June 21, 1964)

chapters.title: Arriving in Laurel
chapters.summary: Sent to Laurel, Mississippi with James 'Jimmy' Garrett and Lester McKinney because the town was too dangerous for white volunteers, Simmons describes basing the trio in Hattiesburg and driving thirty miles north to Laurel to find local hosts. Mrs. Eberhart Spinks opened her door, recognized Simmons as a Freedom Rider, and committed on the spot; Mrs. Carrie Clayton across the street housed the two men.
related_events: Local-host recruitment in Jones County, Mississippi

chapters.title: Becoming Project Director
chapters.summary: When Lester McKinney is jailed in Jones County, Mississippi on a prior charge and barred from returning for five years, James Forman calls Simmons and asks her to become Laurel project director. She accepts on the understanding that a replacement is coming; no one is ever sent and she stays sixteen months. She credits her church, high school, and Girl Scout leadership roles for preparing her.

chapters.title: Freedom Schools and MFDP Organizing
chapters.summary: Simmons describes the Laurel office on Mrs. Clayton's back porch, the eventually rented and rehabilitated nightclub fire-bombed soon after, the Freedom School covering grades 1-12, the Freedom Nursery School, adult literacy classes, and the mock voter registration project that fed the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party state convention. The chapter covers the MFDP delegation's Atlantic City challenge as a turning point that 'jaded' the season SNCC workers.

chapters.title: Staying Past Summer
chapters.summary: Simmons explains her decision to defer Antioch College admission, secured through Staughton Lynd with a recommendation letter from Coretta Scott King, in order to stay in Laurel. Two California women, Lynelle Barrett and Marion Davidson, stayed with her at Mrs. Eberhart Spinks's home, where the three shared a bedroom for another year.

chapters.title: Gender, Race, and Project Leadership
chapters.summary: Simmons reflects on her organizing style as one of three women project directors in Mississippi that summer: ultra-democratic consensus-building, fighting Jackson for resources and good cars, navigating class and race differences with Stanford, Yale, and Goodyear-family volunteers, and instituting an absolute prohibition against sexual harassment after her own pre-orientation sexual assault by a trusted figure.

chapters.title: Violence, Bombings, and the Year After
chapters.summary: Simmons describes the second Laurel office (a duplex shotgun shared with a neighboring family) fire-bombed when a Molotov cocktail diverted the gas line away from the house. She names the post-summer project of six: Ulysses Everett, Charles 'Ben' Hartfield from Hattiesburg expelled from public school, and John Handy from the Mississippi Delta. She describes leading a desegregation attempt at which the Laurel sheriff drew a gun and threatened her.

chapters.title: Mississippi Fairgrounds and Pivot to New York
chapters.summary: Simmons describes the mass arrest of roughly a thousand demonstrators in Jackson, the fifteen-day incarceration at the Mississippi State Fairgrounds in livestock houses with beatings, and two head-on car accidents in the two weeks after her release that prompted James Forman to pull her out to work with the Friends of SNCC in New York. Three months in Harlem expose her to Nation of Islam street preaching, LeRoi Jones's Black Arts Theatre, and Black nationalist thought.
related_events: New York Friends of SNCC work

chapters.title: Atlanta Project and Black Power
chapters.summary: Recruited to Atlanta to run Julian Bond's re-election campaign after he was denied his Georgia House seat, Simmons describes the nucleus that became the SNCC Atlanta Project based in Vine City near the Nation of Islam Mosque #15. Michael Simmons (her future husband), Dwight Williams, and a Mississippi former second lieutenant joined. The group's Wollensak-recorded discussions, typed by Simmons and two other women, became the Black Power position paper later credited to Stokely Carmichael alone.
related_events: Julian Bond Georgia House seat denial and re-election

chapters.title: Whites in SNCC and Expulsion
chapters.summary: Simmons situates the question of white organizers within SNCC's expanded post-1964 ratio, including the Atlanta National Headquarters dynamic where Black projects had to justify resources to predominantly white staff. She describes her 2009-era reconciliation conversation with Bob Zellner about his clan-family background that made SNCC his home. She narrates the Atlanta Project's expulsion from SNCC over a $3,000 check the project refused to relinquish, framed atop deeper disagreement about direction. She also covers the SNCC anti-Vietnam war statement (which she helped draft) and the SNCC Palestinian/Israel statement (which she did not).

chapters.title: AFSC and COINTELPRO Investigation
chapters.summary: As assistant director of the American Friends Service Committee's project on government surveillance and civil liberties, Simmons traveled the country interviewing American Indian Movement, Brown Berets, Black Panther Party, and Rodolfo 'Corky' Gonzales-affiliated organizers about COINTELPRO. Ken Lawrence in Mississippi worked the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission. She visited the Fred Hampton assassination apartment in Chicago and interviewed survivors, learning his bodyguard had drugged them. Documentation lives at AFSC's 1501 Cherry Street, Philadelphia archive.
related_events: Freedom of Information Act file releases

chapters.title: Trauma, Sufism, and Return to Nonviolence
chapters.summary: Simmons reflects on the long-term effects of movement-era trauma, the AFSC trip into Cambodia after the fall of Pol Pot to view the bone-and-skull mountain and interview survivors in Phnom Penh, the assassination of Dr. King while she was at a National Council of Negro Women meeting in Manhattan (running uptown to Harlem where she was caught in a riot), and her 1971 meeting with the Sufi mystic M.R. Bawa Muhaiyaddeen of Sri Lanka. Seventeen years of study brought her around to re-embracing Dr. King's teaching of nonviolence as the only sustainable means of social change.
